coredata.dc:title: An ultrasensitive electrochemiluminescence immunosensor based on zeolitic imidazolate frameworks encapsulating spherical graphite crystals
coredata.prism:issueName: Special issue in honor of Chinese Academician Prof. Hong-Yuan Chen for his 80th birthday
coredata.dc:creator: Zang, Ruhua
He, Ying
Yuan, Ruo
Chai, Yaqin
coredata.dc:description: Here, an ultrasensitive electrochemiluminescence (ECL) immunosensor for the detection of cardiac troponin I (cTnI) was developed by using zeolitic imidazolate frameworks encapsulating spherical graphite crystals (CQDs@ZIF-8) as luminophore. Firstly, luminescent spherical graphite crystals (CQDs) were encapsulated in the zeolitic imidazolate framework (ZIF-8) nanocrystals. To improve the poor conductivity of CQDs@ZIF-8, Au nanoparticles were synthesized on the surface of CQDs@ZIF-8 to form AuNPs/CQDs@ZIF-8. Furthermore, L-cysteine (L-cys) as the coreactant of CQDs was immobilized on the AuNPs to enhance the ECL signal of CQDs. Then, the obtained L-cys/AuNPs/CQDs@ZIF-8 was introduced to construct an ultrasensitive ECL immunosensor for the detection of cTnI. As a result, the proposed immunosensor displayed excellent analytical performances for the detection of cTnI with a linear range from 1fgμL −1 to 10pgμL−1 and a detection limit of 0.33fgμL−1.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Electrochemiluminescence
Immunosensor
Cardiac troponin I
Spherical graphite crystals
Zeolitic imidazolate frameworks

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: ECL detection of cTnI with immunosensor
Stability, selectivity and reproducibility of the immunosensor

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 51473136 and 21575116 ), the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities ( XDJK2014C138 and XDJK2015A002 ) and the National College Students' Innovation Fund of Southwest University under Grant No. 201410635078 .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM image of CQDs (A) and CQDs@ZIF-8 nanocomposites (B) and XRD image of CQDs@ZIF-8 nanocomposites (C).

(A) EIS for (a) the bare GCE; (b) GCE/DpAu; (c) GCE/DpAu/Ab1; (d) GCE/DpAu/Ab1/BSA; and (e) GCE/DpAu/Ab1/BSA/cTnI in 0.1M KCl solution containing 5mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4−. (B) ECL profiles of the immunosensor before (red curve) and after (blue curve) the incubation of Ab2/L-cys/AuNPs/CQDs@ZIF-8 bioconjugates (the immunosensor was incubated with 10pgμL−1 cTnI).
ECL profiles of the proposed ECL immunosensor in different concentrations of cTnI: 0.001, 0.01, 0.05, 0.1, 0.5, 1, 5, and 10pgμL−1 in 0.1M PBS (pH7.4), respectively.

(A) The ECL response of the proposed ECL immunosensor incubated with 0.05pgμL−1 cTnI under continuous cyclic potential scans. (B) The ECL response of the proposed ECL immunosensor incubated with (a) blank, (b) Hb (20pgμL−1), (c) AFP (20pgμL−1), (d) cTnI (0.5pgμL−1), (e) a mixture containing cTnI (0.5pgμL−1), Hb (20pgμL−1), (f) a mixture containing cTnI (0.5pgμL−1), AFP (20pgμL−1), and (g) a mixture containing cTnI (0.5pgμL−1), Hb (20pgμL−1), AFP (20pgμL−1). Scanning from −2 to 0V at a scan rate of 100mVs−1.
ECL profiles of CQDs separately, with L-cys and with S2O8 2−.
Illustration of the preparation of Ab2/L-cys/AuNPs/CQDs@ZIF-8 bioconjugates (A); the fabrication of the proposed ECL immunosensor and the reacted mechanism (B).
Comparison of our research with other methods for cTnI detection.

head.ce:title: An ultrasensitive electrochemiluminescence immunosensor based on zeolitic imidazolate frameworks encapsulating spherical graphite crystals

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Luminescent and Real-Time Analytical Chemistry, Ministry of Education, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, People's Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Luminescent and Real-Time Analytical Chemistry
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Here, an ultrasensitive electrochemiluminescence (ECL) immunosensor for the detection of cardiac troponin I (cTnI) was developed by using zeolitic imidazolate frameworks encapsulating spherical graphite crystals (CQDs@ZIF-8) as luminophore. Firstly, luminescent spherical graphite crystals (CQDs) were encapsulated in the zeolitic imidazolate framework (ZIF-8) nanocrystals. To improve the poor conductivity of CQDs@ZIF-8, Au nanoparticles were synthesized on the surface of CQDs@ZIF-8 to form AuNPs/CQDs@ZIF-8. Furthermore, L-cysteine (L-cys) as the coreactant of CQDs was immobilized on the AuNPs to enhance the ECL signal of CQDs. Then, the obtained L-cys/AuNPs/CQDs@ZIF-8 was introduced to construct an ultrasensitive ECL immunosensor for the detection of cTnI. As a result, the proposed immunosensor displayed excellent analytical performances for the detection of cTnI with a linear range from 1fgμL −1 to 10pgμL−1 and a detection limit of 0.33fgμL−1.

ce:section.ce:para: Cardiac troponin I (cTnI), as a specialized myocardial regulatory protein, has been acknowledged as an important principle diagnostic biomarker to monitor acute myocardial infarction (AMI) which is a serious threat to the public health [1–6]. However, the traditional detection methods are quietly limited to expensive instrument, long detecting time or low sensitivity. Therefore, it is very meaningful to develop an ultrasensitive determination method of cTnI for early assessment of the severity and progression rate of AMI.

Electrochemiluminescence (ECL), as a powerful detection tool combined the merits of electrochemistry and chemiluminescence, has attracted great attentions due to its high sensitivity, simple format and rapid response in recent years [7–11]. However, traditional ECL luminophores were hard to be immobilized and the luminescence efficiency of traditional ECL luminophores was not enough for ultrasensitive detection. Therefore, the key points to enhance the sensitivity of ECL system are developing new luminophores with high luminescence efficiency and immobilizing these well performed luminophores efficiently. Up to now, several kinds of novel luminophores have been introduced into the constructions of ECL systems, such as noble-metal nanoclusters [12], iridium complex [13], semiconductor quantum dots [14–16] and spherical graphite crystals [17–19]. Among these luminophores, spherical graphite crystals with good biological compatibility, high luminescence efficiency and low cost have been widely reported in the researches of ECL analytical technologies. However, spherical graphite crystals are difficult to be immobilized on the electrode, which limits its application in ECL biosensor. Pillai's group has reported a strategy based on zeolitic imidazolate framework (ZIF-8) nanocrystals encapsulating fluorescent spherical graphite crystals (CQDs@ZIF-8) [20]. However, the poor conductivity limited the application of CQDs@ZIF-8 in ECL system. Based on this strategy, Au nanoparticles (AuNPs) were synthesized on the surface of the CQDs@ZIF-8 to form AuNPs/CQDs@ZIF-8 with improved electric conductivity.

Inspired by all of these perspectives, an ultrasensitive ECL immunosensor for the detection of cTnI was developed based on CQDs@ZIF-8. Firstly, AuNPs were synthesized on the surface of the CQDs@ZIF-8 nanocomposite. Then, L-cysteine (L-cys) as the coreactant of CQDs was immobilized on the AuNPs to enhance the ECL signal of CQDs. Subsequently, the detection antibodies (Ab2) were immobilized on the AuNPs through AuN bonds to form Ab2 bioconjugates (Ab2/L-cys/AuNPs/CQDs@ZIF-8). At the same time, AuNPs were electrodeposited on the glass carbon electrode (GCE) forming a porous AuNPs film (DpAu) to fix the capture antibodies (Ab1). Finally, through special sandwiched immunoreactions with cTnI, the obtained Ab2 bioconjugates could be modified on the electrode surface through sandwiched immunoreactions with cTnI and provide a significantly enhanced ECL signal. In the construction of this ECL immunosensor, L-cys could increase the ECL intensity of the CQDs and improve the sensitivity of the proposed ECL immunosensor. Therefore, the preparation of CQDs@ZIF-8 boarded the application of CQDs in ECL system and proposed an effective way for the immobilization of CQDs. And the proposed immunosensor might hold a new promise tool for sensitive cTnI detection.

Potassium persulfate (K2S2O8) and zinc nitrate (Zn(NO3)2·6H2O) were purchased from Kelong Chemical Company (Chengdu, China). Gold chloride (HAuCl4) was purchased from Kangda Company (Shanghai, China). L-cysteine (L-cys), sodium borohydride (NaBH4), citric acid (CA), 2-methylimidazole, bovine serum albumin (BSA) were purchased from Sigma Chemical Co. (St. Louis, MO, USA). Hemoglobin (Hb), cTnI antibody (anti-cTnI), cTnI, and α-fetoprotein (AFP) were bought from Biocell Company (Zhengzhou, China). Phosphate buffered solution (PBS) (pH7.4, 0.1M) was prepared with 0.1M Na2HPO4, 0.1M KH2PO4 and 0.1M KCl. All chemicals were of analytical grade and used without further purification. All solutions were prepared with ultrapure water (18.2MΩ) and stored in the refrigerator (4°C) when no use.

The ECL emission was monitored with a model MPI-A electrochemiluminescence analyzer (Xi'an Remax Electronic Science & Technology Co. Ltd., Xi'an, China) with the voltage of the photomultiplier tube (PMT) at 800V and the potential range from −2.0 to 0V with a scan rate of 100mVs−1. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements were performed with a CHI 660A electrochemical workstation (Shanghai Chenhua Instrument, China). All the ECL and electrochemical experiments were carried out with a three-electrode system: an Ag/AgCl (sat. KCl) as reference electrode, a platinum wire as counter electrode, and the modified glassy carbon electrode (GCE) as working electrode, respectively. The morphologies of the nanomaterials were characterized by the transmission electron microscope (TEM, H600, Hitachi Instrument, Japan).

The preparation of CQDs was according to a previous literature with a little modification [21]. Firstly, 4g CA was added into a 10mL beaker and heated to 200°C. When the CA was liquated, the color of the liquid was changed from colorless to pale yellow, and then became orange after about 30min, indicating the successful synthesis of CQDs. The obtained orange liquid was diluted to 100mL with ultrapure water and neutralized to pH7.0 with NaOH (0.25M).

The GQD@ZIF-8 nanocomposite was prepared according to a previous literature with some modifications [20]. Firstly, 2-methylimidazole (200mg), Zn(NO3)2·6H2O (95mg) and 100μL as-synthesized CQDs were dissolved in 15mL methanol and then the mixture was undisturbed for about 24h at room temperature. Subsequently, the resulting white precipitate was collected by centrifugation. The production was washed several times with methanol to get GQD@ZIF-8 nanocomposite.

The Ab2/L-cys/AuNPs/CQDs@ZIF-8 bioconjugates were synthesized based on the following steps (Scheme 1A). Firstly, HAuCl4 was added into 2mL GQD@ZIF-8 nanocomposite with magnet stirring. Several minutes later, the AuCl4 − were absorbed onto the surface of CQDs@ZIF-8. Then, NaBH4 was added into the mixture to reduce AuCl4 −, forming Au nanoparticles (AuNPs) on the surface of CQDs@ZIF-8 (AuNPs/CQDs@ZIF-8). Then, a proper quantity of L-cys was added into the solution under gently stirring for about 12h (L-cys/AuNPs/CQDs@ZIF-8). Subsequently, Ab2 was added to the mixture and slightly stirred for 12h at 4°C. Finally, 100μL BSA solution (1%, wt/wt) was implemented to block the non-specific adsorption of the AuNPs with the reaction time of 20min. At last, the sandwiched Ab2 bioconjugates (Ab2/L-cys/AuNPs/CQDs@ZIF-8 bioconjugates), were redispersed in PBS (pH7.4) and stored in 4°C for further use.

The fabrication of the ECL immunosensor was shown in Scheme 1B. Firstly, the glassy carbon electrode (GCE, Φ =4mm) was polished with 0.3, 0.05μm alumina powder respectively to obtain a mirror-like surface. And then the electrode was sonicated separately in ethanol and ultrapure water. Subsequently, the electrode was immersed in 2mL of 1% HAuCl4 solution for electrochemical deposition under constant potential of −0.2V for 30s to obtain DpAu. Then 15μL capture antibodies (Ab1) solution were dropped onto the surface of DpAu for 12h incubation at 4°C. Next, 10μL BSA solution was used to block the non-specific binding sites on DpAu Subsequently, 10μL cTnI standard solution was incubated on the modified electrode. Finally, 15μL Ab2 bioconjugates solution was incubated on the modified electrode for 30min. Ultimately, the obtained ECL immunosensor was stored at 4°C before used. After every modified step, washing with ultrapure water was utilized to remove excess reagents.

The size and morphologies of the synthesized CQDs and CQDs@ZIF-8 nanocomposites were characterized by TEM. As shown in Fig. 1(A), the CQDs were monodisperse nanoparticles with a diameter of 5nm. As shown in Fig. 1(B), the CQDs@ZIF-8 nanocomposites were separated nanoparticles with a diameter of 180nm. As shown in Fig. 1(C), the XRD patterns of CQDs@ZIF-8 were similar to the ZIF-8 used in the reference [17], which indicated the successful synthetization of CQDs@ZIF-8 nanoparticles.

The fabrication process of the ECL immunosensor was characterized by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) in 5mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− containing 0.1M KCl. Fig. 2(A) showed the EIS of stepwise modified processes of the proposed electrode. A small semicircle was observed for the bare GCE (curve a). When AuNPs were deposited on the GCE, the electron transfer resistance (R et) was decreased (curve b) due to the good conductivity of AuNPs. When Ab1, BSA, cTnI antigen were incubated onto the electrode, the R et was increased respectively (curves c, d, e), because these proteins might hinder the electron transfer significantly.

Furthermore, the proposed immunosensor was characterized by ECL in PBS (0.1M, pH7.4). As shown in Fig. 2(B), a weak ECL response before the incubation of the Ab2 bioconjugate (red curve) due to the ECL of peroxydisulfate solution [22]. However, due to the existence of CQDs@ZIF-8, the ECL signal was greatly improved when the Ab2 bioconjugate was immobilized on the electrode (blue curve).

ce:section.ce:section-title: ECL detection of cTnI with immunosensor
ce:section.ce:para:
The detecting performance of the proposed immunosensor was evaluated by incubating with different concentrations of cTnI (Fig. 3 ). The ECL intensity increased with the increasing of cTnI concentration with the linear range for proposed immunosensor from 1fgμL−1 to 10pgμL−1 and a detection limit of 0.33fgμL−1 (S/N=3). The regression equation was I =1253.9 log c
cTnI +4893.0 (where I was the ECL intensity and c
cTnI was the concentration of cTnI). The results in Table 1 allow those obtained with the proposed immunosensor to be compared with those previously reported. The results indicated that the proposed immunosensor could be an appropriate tool for the ultrasensitive detection of cTnI.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability, selectivity and reproducibility of the immunosensor
ce:section.ce:para: The stability of the ECL responses of the immunosensor was evaluated under continuous cyclic potential scans. As shown in Fig. 4(A), there were no obvious changes for ECL signal, indicating the good stability of the proposed immunosensor.

The selectivity and specificity were evaluated with the assistance of some possible interfering substances, such as Hb and AFP. As shown in Fig. 4(B), the ECL signal was similar to that of the blank sample when the immunosensor was incubated in the Hb or AFP solution. And no obvious changes were observed on comparing the ECL of 0.5pgμL−1 cTnI with/without interfering agents, which indicated that the immunosensor possessed good selectivity for the detection of cTnI.

The relative standard deviations (RSDs) of intra- and inter-assays were utilized to assess the reproducibility of the proposed immunosensor. Neither of the RSDs were more than 5%, which illustrated that the proposed immunosensor had acceptable reproducibility.

To study the amplified effect of co-reactant for CQDs, the L-cys and S2O8 2− were added into the aqueous solution of CQDs. As it was presented in Fig. 5 , the L-cys and S2O8 2− could both enhance the ECL signal of CQDs. And the enhancement of S2O8 2− were stronger than L-cys.

The recovery experiment was performed by standard addition methods in human serum to monitor the feasibility of the ECL immunosensor. A satisfactory recovery in the range of 93.7–107% was received, which indicated that our strategy is a promising tool for cTnI detection in real samples.

In summary, an ultrasensitive ECL immunosensor for the detection of cardiac troponin I (cTnI) was proposed by using CQDs@ZIF-8 as luminophore with L-cys and peroxydisulfate as co-reactant. The proposed immunosensor demonstrated excellent performance for the detection of cTnI with high sensitivity, wide linear range, good stability and selectivity. Furthermore, the proposed immunosensor provided a novel strategy to introduce CQDs into the fabrication of ECL biosensor and the proposed immunosensor might provide a promising tool for detecting cTnI in biomedical research and clinical applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51473136 and 21575116), the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (XDJK2014C138 and XDJK2015A002) and the National College Students' Innovation Fund of Southwest University under Grant No. 201410635078.
